nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of seaward reef slopes and deep lagoons, on mixed coral and rubble patches, at depths of roughly 3-35 m.

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild, feeding on zooplankton near the substrate. In aquariums, generally accepts a varied diet of mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats, herbivore preparations, and quality flakes or pellets fed two to three times daily.

avoidWith: very docile fish that share the same niche
small slow movers like firefish in confined tanks

summary: A small, hardy Indo-Pacific damselfish with a canary-yellow head and pale lavender-blue body. Generally considered reef-safe and one of the more tolerant damsels, though tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: Talbot's Damselfish is often recommended as one of the more peaceable damsels and is commonly considered beginner-friendly. It tends to be more aggressive in undersized tanks or when kept with very similar species, so a longer footprint with multiple defensible territories in the rockwork is generally advised. Although sometimes sold as suitable for 30-US-gallon (~114 L) tanks, biasing toward a ~120 L or larger system with an open swimming area tends to reduce conspecific and inter-species aggression as the fish matures. Generally regarded as reef-safe with corals, but hobbyists have reported occasional harassment of small ornamental shrimp and hermit crabs, so caution is warranted with delicate or newly added invertebrates. Sources gave a temperature span of roughly 22-28°C across captive and wild data; the 23-27°C range used here reflects the averaged hobbyist target and overlaps the FishBase wild range (24.9-28.9°C). Lifespan estimates vary between sources (3-5 years vs 5-7 years); 4-6 years is used as a midpoint.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pair bonding during breeding. Eggs are demersal and adhere to a chosen substrate, with males guarding and aerating the nest until hatching. Captive breeding has been documented, but rearing the pelagic larvae is generally considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive, reef compatible, max 2.25 in.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria/misc), max size 6.0 cm TL, depth 3-35 m, wild temperature 24.9-28.9°C, Indo-West Pacific distribution, planktivorous trophic level 2.8, oviparous with male egg care.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-check: 2.5 in max, 72-82°F, pH 8.0-8.5, SG 1.020-1.025, 30 gal min, reef safe, lifespan 3-5 yr, Indo-Pacific to Fiji range. Slightly wider temperature and pH bands than LiveAquaria; values were averaged into the final ranges.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn on a cleaned substrate; males guard and fan the demersal eggs. Larvae are pelagic and historically considered difficult to rear, though limited captive success has been reported.
